blocks:
Ayahs

يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْمَلِكِ ٱلْقُدُّوسِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (١)
Whatever there is in the heavens and in the earth glorifies Allah, the Sovereign, the All-holy, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى بَعَثَ فِى ٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَإِن كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢)
It is He who sent to the unlettered [people] an apostle from among themselves, to recite to them His signs, to purify them, and to teach them the Book and wisdom, and earlier they had indeed been in manifest error. (2)
وَءَاخَرِينَ مِنْهُمْ لَمَّا يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِمْ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣)
And to others from among them [as well] who have not yet joined them. And He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (3)
ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٤)
That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (4)

Content of Surah al-Jumu'ah

This Surah essentially revolves around two main axes: first, attention to Monotheism (Tawhid), the Attributes of God, the purpose of the mission (Ba'thah) of the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله, and the issue of the Resurrection (Ma'ad); and second, the constructive program of the Friday Prayer and some of the characteristics of this great act of worship.

However, from another perspective, the content of this Surah can be summarized into several sections:

  1. The universal glorification (Tasbih) of all beings.
  2. The purpose of the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله from the dimension of education and purification.
  3. A warning to the believers not to deviate from the principles of the true religion as the Jews did.
  4. An allusion to the universal law of death, which is a gateway to the world of eternity.
  5. Emphatic instructions for performing the obligatory Friday Prayer and suspending business and trade to participate in it.

Virtue of Reciting Surah al-Jumu'ah

Many narrations have been recorded regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, whether independently or within the daily prayers.

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever recites Surah al-Jumu'ah, Allah will grant him ten good deeds (hasanat) equal to the number of those who attend the Friday Prayer and those who do not attend it in all the cities of the Muslims." [^1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "It is necessary for every believer among our followers (Shi'a) to recite Surah al-Jumu'ah and 'Sabbih Isma Rabbika al-A'la' on Thursday night (the eve of Friday), and to recite Surah al-Jumu'ah and al-Munafiqun during the Friday noon prayer. When one does this, it is as if he has performed the action of the Messenger of Allah صلي الله عليه وآله, and his reward and recompense from Allah is Paradise." [^2]

Particular emphasis has been placed on reciting Surah al-Jumu'ah and al-Munafiqun in the Friday Prayer, and some of these narrations state that they should not be omitted if possible. [^3] Although deviating from Surah al-Tawhid and 'Qul Ya Ayyuha al-Kafirun' to other Surahs during prayer recitation is generally not permitted, this issue is an exception specifically for the Friday Prayer; deviating from them to Surah al-Jumu'ah and al-Munafiqun is permitted and even considered recommended (Mustahabb).

All of these are signs of the extraordinary importance of this Surah of the Holy Quran.

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah al-Jumu'ah; Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 320. [^3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 321.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ الْمَلِكِ الْقُدُّوسِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ (1) هُوَ الَّذِي بَعَثَ فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ رَسُولاً مِنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِهِ وَ يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَ يُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ وَ إِنْ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ (2) وَ آخَرِينَ مِنْهُمْ لَمَّا يَلْحَقُوا بِهِمْ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ (3) ذلِكَ فَضْلُ اللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ (4)

Commentary

The Purpose of the Prophet's Mission

This Surah also begins with the glorification and sanctification of the Lord, referring to a portion of His Attributes of Beauty and Majesty and His Most Beautiful Names (Asma al-Husna), which in reality serves as an introduction to the upcoming discussions.

It says: "Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth constantly glorifies Allah, and through the language of their state and their speech, they deem Him pure from all deficiencies and defects" (يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ).

"The same God who is the Sovereign and the Ruler, and is free from every defect and flaw" (الْمَلِكِ الْقُدُّوسِ).

"The God who is All-Mighty and All-Wise" (الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ).

In this way, He first emphasizes "Ownership and Sovereignty" and then "His being sanctified from any kind of oppression, tyranny, or deficiency." This is because, given the countless injustices of kings and monarchs, the term "King" (Malik) might evoke unholy connotations, all of which are washed away by the word "Holy" (Quddus).

On the other hand, He relies on "Power" and "Knowledge," which are the two main pillars of governance. As we shall see, these attributes have a close connection to the future discussions of this Surah, showing that the selection of Divine Attributes in various verses of the Quran is based on a specific calculation, order, and relationship.

Regarding the universal glorification by the beings of the world, we have previously had detailed discussions. [^1]

After this brief and meaningful reference to the issue of Monotheism and the Attributes of God, the Surah addresses the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله and the goal of this great messengership, which is in connection with Allah being All-Mighty, All-Wise, and Holy: "He is the One Who raised among the unlettered a Messenger from among themselves to recite His verses to them" (هُوَ الَّذِي بَعَثَ فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ رَسُولًا مِنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِهِ).

And in the light of this recitation, "to purify them from every kind of polytheism, disbelief, deviation, and corruption, and to teach them the Book and Wisdom" (وَ يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَ يُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ).

"Even though before that they were in manifest error" (وَ إِنْ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ).

Interestingly, the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله, with those characteristics that cannot be explained except through miracles, is taken as a sign of God's greatness and a proof of His existence, saying:

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 12, under verse 44 of Surah al-Isra, and under verse 41 of Surah al-Nur (Vol. 14, p. 497).

Allah is the very One Who appointed such a Prophet and brought about such a masterpiece in creation! "Ummiyyin" is the plural of "Ummi," meaning unlettered (attributed to "Umm" (mother), meaning he has seen no school other than the school of his mother's lap). Some have considered it to mean "the people of Mecca," because Mecca was called "Umm al-Qura" (the Mother of Settlements), but this possibility seems unlikely, as the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله was not sent only to the people of Mecca, nor was Surah al-Jumu'ah revealed in Mecca.

Some commentators have also interpreted it to mean "the Arab nation" in contrast to the Jews and others, citing verse 75 of Surah Al-Imran as evidence, which says: "The Jews said: 'We have no responsibility toward the unlettered (non-Jews).'"

Even if we accept this interpretation, it is because the Jews considered themselves "People of the Book" and literate, while viewing the Arab nation as illiterate and uneducated.

Therefore, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all. [^1] It is noteworthy that it says the Prophet of Islam rose from among this very unlettered group and class to clarify the greatness of his mission and to be a proof of his truthfulness. For a book like the Quran with such deep and vast content, and a culture like the culture of Islam, it is impossible to be the product of human thought—especially a human who was himself unlettered and was not raised in an environment of science and knowledge. This is a light that rose from darkness, and a lush garden that emerged from the heart of the desert, and this itself is a manifest miracle and a clear document of his truthfulness.

[^1]: Regarding the meaning of "Ummi," we have provided a more detailed explanation under verse 157 of Surah al-A'raf (refer to Vol. 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 400).

In the above verse, the goal of this mission is summarized in three matters: one has a preliminary aspect, which is the recitation of the Divine verses; and the other two parts, namely "the refinement and purification of souls" and "the teaching of the Book and Wisdom," constitute the two great final goals.

Yes, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله came to train humans in both the fields of science and knowledge, and ethics and action, so that they may fly to the heights of the heaven of happiness using these two wings, take the path toward Allah, and attain the station of His proximity.

It is also worth noting that in some verses of the Quran, "purification" (tazkiyah) is mentioned before "teaching" (ta'lim), and in some, "teaching" is mentioned before "purification." That is, out of four instances, in three cases training precedes teaching, and in one case teaching precedes training.

This expression, while showing that these two matters have a mutual influence on each other (ethics is born of knowledge just as knowledge is born of ethics), specifies the primacy of training. Of course, the intention is true knowledge, not conventional terms in the guise of science.

The difference between "the Book" and "Wisdom" (Hikmah) might be that the first refers to the Quran and the second to the words of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and his teachings, which are called "Sunnah."

It is also possible that "the Book" refers to the fundamental instructions of Islam, and "Wisdom" refers to their philosophy and secrets.

It is also noteworthy that "Wisdom" (Hikmah) originally means to prevent for the purpose of reform; the bit of a horse's bridle is called "hikmah" because it prevents and restrains it, keeping it on the correct path. Therefore, its concept refers to rational proofs. From here it becomes clear that mentioning the Book and Wisdom one after the other can refer to the two great sources of cognition, namely "Revelation" and "Reason."

Or in other words, the heavenly laws and Islamic teachings, while originating from Divine revelation, are measurable and understandable by the scale of reason (referring to the general principles of the laws).

As for "manifest error" (ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ) mentioned at the end of the verse as the background of the Arab people, it is a concise and meaningful allusion to the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), where deviation ruled over their entire society. What error could be worse and more manifest than this:

They worshipped idols that they carved from stone and wood with their own hands, and sought refuge in these mindless beings for their problems.

They buried their daughters alive with their own hands; moreover, they even boasted and took pride in this act, saying they did not let their honor fall into the hands of strangers!

Their prayer and worship ceremonies consisted of clapping and whistling next to the House of the Kaaba, and even women would circumambulate the House of God completely naked and consider it an act of worship! All kinds of superstitions and delusions dominated their thoughts, and war, bloodshed, and pillaging were their sources of pride. Women among them were worthless commodities upon whom they even gambled! They were deprived of the simplest human rights. Hatreds and enmities were passed down as inheritance from fathers to children, and for this reason, bloodshed and killing were considered ordinary matters.

Yes, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله came and saved them from this manifest error by the blessing of the Book and Wisdom, and educated and purified them. Truly, penetrating such a lost society is itself one of the proofs of the greatness of Islam and a manifest miracle of our great Prophet.

However, since the Prophet of Islam was not sent only to this "unlettered" people, but rather his call encompassed all the people of the world, the next verse adds: "He is also sent to another group of believers who have not yet joined them" (وَ آخَرِينَ مِنْهُمْ لَمَّا يَلْحَقُوا بِهِمْ). [^1] Other nations that came into existence after the companions of the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله were trained in the school of the Prophet's education and were quenched by the pure spring of the Quran and the Muhammadan Sunnah صلي الله عليه وآله. Yes, they too were included in this great call.

In this way, the above verse includes all nations that came into being after the companions of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, whether Arab or non-Arab ('Ajam). In a Hadith, we read that when the Noble Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله recited this verse, they asked: "Who are these people?"

The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله placed his hand on Salman's shoulder and said: "If faith were at the Pleiades (a distant star used as a proverb in this context), men from among these people (Persians) would surely attain it." [^2]

And since all these matters stem from the power and wisdom of God,

[^1]: "Others" (akharin) is conjoined to "the unlettered" (ummiyyin), and the pronoun "from them" (minhum) refers to "the believers," which is understood from the context. Some have also suggested it is conjoined to the pronoun in "He teaches them" (yu'allimuhum), but the first meaning is more appropriate. [^2]: This Hadith is narrated by Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur, Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf, Qurtubi in his Tafsir, Maraghi in his Tafsir, and Sayyid Qutb in Fi Zilal under the verse in question; it is originally taken from Sahih Bukhari.

at the end of the verse He says: "And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

Then, referring to this great blessing, namely the mission of the Noble Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله and his program of education and purification, He adds: "That is the grace of Allah which He grants to whom He wills and deems worthy, and Allah is the Possessor of Great Grace" (ذلِكَ فَضْلُ اللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ).

In reality, this verse is like verse 164 of Surah Al-Imran which says: "Certainly Allah conferred a great favor upon the believers when He raised among them a Messenger from among themselves, reciting to them His verses and purifying them and teaching them the Book and Wisdom, although before they were in manifest error," and almost everything in it is similar to the verses under discussion.

Some have also suggested that "That is the grace of Allah" refers to the original station of Prophethood, which Allah grants to whoever He deems worthy.

However, the first interpretation is more appropriate, although combining both is also possible: both the leadership of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله for the Ummah is a Divine grace, and the station of Prophethood for the person of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله himself.

It goes without saying that the expression "whom He wills" (man yasha') does not mean that God grants His grace and mercy to someone without calculation; rather, the "will" (mashiyyah) here is coupled with "wisdom," just as the description of God as All-Mighty and All-Wise in the first verse of the Surah clarified this point.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, also says in Nahj al-Balagha, explaining this great Divine grace: "Look at the sites of Allah's blessings upon them when He sent them a Messenger, who secured their obedience to his religion and united them upon his call. See how this great blessing spread the wing of its dignity over them, and made the streams of its bounty flow for them, and the religion encompassed them in its beneficial blessings. Thus, they became drowned in its favors and delighted in the freshness of its life." [^1]

Key Point: Divine Grace is Based on Calculation

In a Hadith, it is mentioned that a group of the poor of the Ummah came to the Messenger of Allah صلي الله عليه وآله and said: "O Messenger of Allah! The wealthy have wealth to spend and we have nothing; they have the means for Hajj and we do not; they have the resources to free slaves and we do not."

The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said: "Whoever says 'Allahu Akbar' a hundred times, it is better than freeing a slave; and whoever says 'Subhan Allah' a hundred times, it is better than saddling and bridling a hundred horses for Jihad; and whoever says 'La ilaha illa Allah' a hundred times, his deed is superior to all people on that day, unless someone says more."

This word reached the wealthy; they also went after these recitations (dhikr). The poor of the Ummah came back to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and said: "Your words have reached

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 192 (Sermon of al-Qasi'ah).

these people and they too have started the dhikr!" The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said: "That is the grace of Allah which He grants to whom He wills" (ذلِكَ فَضْلُ اللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ). (Referring to the fact that this is for the likes of you who have the longing to spend but do not have the means, while for those who are wealthy, the way to reach Divine grace is through spending from their wealth). [^1] This Hadith is also a witness to what we said above: that Divine grace is based on a wise calculation.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 284.

Ayahs

مَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوهَا كَمَثَلِ ٱلْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًۢا ۚ بِئْسَ مَثَلُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥)
The example of those who were charged with the Torah, then failed to carry it, is that of an ass carrying books. Evil is the example of the people who deny Allah’s signs, and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (5)
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوٓا۟ إِن زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ لِلَّهِ مِن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٦)
Say, ‘O Jews! If you claim that you are Allah’s favourites, to the exclusion of other people, then long for death, should you be truthful.’ (6)
وَلَا يَتَمَنَّوْنَهُۥٓ أَبَدًۢا بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٧)
Yet they will never long for it, because of what their hands have sent ahead, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers. (7)
قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْمَوْتَ ٱلَّذِى تَفِرُّونَ مِنْهُ فَإِنَّهُۥ مُلَـٰقِيكُمْ ۖ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٨)
Say, ‘The death that you flee will indeed encounter you. Then you will be returned to the Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, and He will inform you about what you used to do.’ (8)

Commentary

A Beast Carrying Books!

In some narrations, it is mentioned that the Jews used to say: "If Muhammad has been sent as a Messenger, his mission does not include us." Therefore, the first verse under discussion reminds them that if they had accurately read and acted upon their own Heavenly Book, they would not have said such a thing, for the glad tidings of the appearance of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) are contained within it.

He says: "The likeness of those who were entrusted with the Torah, then did not uphold it, is as the likeness of a donkey that carries volumes [of books]" (مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا التَّوْراةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوها كَمَثَلِ الْحِمارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفاراً).

The donkey feels nothing from the books except their weight; it makes no difference to it whether it carries stones and wood on its back or books containing the most precise secrets of creation and the most beneficial programs for life.

This self-satisfied people, who contented themselves only with the name of the Torah or its recitation without reflecting on its content or acting upon it, are just like this animal, which is proverbial for its stupidity and ignorance among both the elite and the common folk.

This is the most eloquent example that can be given for a scholar without action: one who carries the weight of the responsibility of knowledge on his shoulders without benefiting from its blessings. Those who deal only with the words of the Quran but are unaware of its content and practical program (and how many such individuals there are among the ranks of Muslims!) are included in this verse.

There is also the possibility that upon hearing the initial verses of this Surah and the like, which speak of the blessing of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) mission, the Jews might have said: "We too are People of the Book, and we take pride in the mission of Prophet Moses, the Interlocutor of Allah (Kalim)." The Quran replies to them: "What benefit is there, when you have trampled the commands of the Torah underfoot and never implemented them in your lives?"

In any case, this is a warning to all Muslims to be careful not to meet a fate like that of the Jews. This great divine grace that has encompassed them, and this Magnificent Quran that has been revealed to them, is not meant merely to gather dust in houses, or to be worn as an "amulet against the evil eye," or to be passed under for protection from accidents during travel, or to be sent to a new house along with a "mirror" and a "broom" for good luck! To degrade it to such an extent, or for their ultimate ambition to be limited to efforts in Tajweed, beautiful recitation, Tarteel, and memorization, while it has the least reflection in their individual and social lives and no trace of it is seen in their beliefs and actions.

Then, continuing this parable, He adds: "Wretched is the likeness of the people who deny the signs of Allah" (بِئْسَ مَثَلُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ).

How could they not be likened to a "donkey carrying volumes"? For they denied the divine signs not only through their actions but also with their tongues. As we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 87, regarding this same Jewish people: أَ فَكُلَّما جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ بِما لا تَهْوى‌ أَنْفُسُكُمُ اسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ فَفَرِيقاً كَذَّبْتُمْ وَ فَرِيقاً تَقْتُلُونَ‌: "Is it that whenever there came to you a messenger with what your souls did not desire, you grew arrogant? Some you denied and some you killed?"

At the end of the verse, in a short and meaningful sentence, He says: "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

It is true that guidance is the work of Allah, but it requires a prerequisite. That prerequisite—which is the spirit of seeking truth—must be provided by the humans themselves, and the wrongdoers are far from this stage.

We mentioned that the Jews considered themselves a chosen nation, a "separate breed," so to speak. Sometimes they even claimed to be the sons of God! And at times they portrayed themselves as the special friends of Allah, as stated in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 18: وَ قالَتِ الْيَهُودُ وَ النَّصارى‌ نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللَّهِ وَ أَحِبَّاؤُهُ‌: "The Jews and the Christians say, 'We are the children of Allah and His beloved ones'" (even if they meant it metaphorically).

In response to these baseless ambitions, especially from a group that carried the Divine Book but did not act upon it, the Quran says: "Say, 'O you who are Jews, if you claim that you are the allies of Allah to the exclusion of [other] people, then wish for death, if you are truthful'" (قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ هادُوا إِنْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءُ لِلَّهِ مِنْ دُونِ النَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا الْمَوْتَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ) [1]. This is because a friend always longs to meet the friend, and we know that the spiritual meeting

[1]: مِنْ دُونِ النَّاسِ according to some commentators is a "state" (hal) for the noun of "Inna," and according to others, it is an adjective for "Awliya."

with the Lord occurs in the Hereafter, when the veils of the worldly realm are cast aside, the dust of lusts and whims settles, the curtains are lifted, and man sees the heart-ravishing beauty of the Beloved with the eye of the heart, stepping onto the carpet of His proximity, and finding a way into the sanctuary of the Friend, as exemplified by: "فِي مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ مَلِيكٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ" (In a seat of honor near a Sovereign, Perfect in Ability).

If you are truthful and are His special friends, then why are you so attached to the life of this world?! Why are you so terrified of death? This is proof that you are not sincere in this claim of yours.

The Quran expresses this same meaning in another way in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 96, saying: وَ لَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ النَّاسِ عَلى‌ حَياةٍ وَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ وَ ما هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِ مِنَ الْعَذابِ أَنْ يُعَمَّرُ وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ‌: "And you will surely find them the most greedy of people for life—even [more] than those who associate others with Allah. One of them wishes that he could be granted a life of a thousand years, but it would not remove him in the least from the [coming] punishment that he should be granted life. And Allah is Seeing of what they do."

Then, pointing to the main reason for their fear of death, He adds: "But they will never wish for it, because of what their hands have put forth" (وَ لا يَتَمَنَّوْنَهُ أَبَداً بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ). But "Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِالظَّالِمِينَ).

In reality, a person's fear of death is due to one of two factors: either they do not believe in life after death and perceive death as a monster of annihilation and the dark abode of non-existence—and it is natural for a human to flee from non-existence.

Or, they believe in the world after death but see their record of deeds as so dark and black that they are severely afraid of appearing in that great court.

Since the Jews believed in the Resurrection and the world after death, the cause of their terror of death was naturally the second factor.

The term "wrongdoers" (ظالمين) has a broad meaning that encompasses all the improper actions of the Jews, from killing the great prophets of Allah to making false attributions to them, usurping rights, plundering wealth, looting people's capital, and being tainted with various moral corruptions.

But certainly, this terror and anxiety do not solve any problem; death is a camel that kneels at everyone's door. Therefore, the Quran says: "O Prophet! Say, 'Indeed, the death from which you flee—indeed, it will meet you'" (قُلْ إِنَّ الْمَوْتَ الَّذِي تَفرُّونَ مِنْهُ فَإِنَّهُ مُلاقِيكُمْ). "Then you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, and He will inform you of what you used to do" (ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلى‌ عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

The law of death is among the most universal and widespread laws of this world; the great prophets of Allah and the near-stationed angels all die, and nothing remains in this world except the Pure Essence of Allah: "كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْها فانٍ وَ يَبْقى‌ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو الْجَلالِ وَ الْإِكْرامِ" (Surah Ar-Rahman, verses 26 and 27).

Both death is a certain law of this world, and the presence in the court of Allah's justice and the auditing of deeds is certain, and Allah is precisely aware of all the deeds of His servants.

Therefore, the only way to end this terror is to purify one's deeds and

wash the heart from the pollution of sin; for he whose account is clean, what fear has he of the audit? Only in this case can one say like Ali (peace be upon him):

"Far from it! After all these trials and tribulations, by Allah, the son of Abu Talib is more intimate with death than an infant is with its mother's breast!" [1] And when his blessed forehead was split open by the blow of the "most wretched of the later generations," he let out a cry and said:

"I have succeeded, by the Lord of the Kaaba!"

Key Points

1- A Scholar Without Action?

Undoubtedly, the pursuit of knowledge involves many difficulties, but no matter how great these difficulties are, they are insignificant compared to the blessings derived from knowledge. A person's misery begins on the day they endure the hardship of acquiring knowledge but gain none of its blessings. Such a person is exactly like a beast that feels the weight of a load of books on its back without benefiting from their content.

In some expressions, a scholar without action is likened to a "fruitless tree" (شجر بلا ثمر) or a "rainless cloud" (سحاب بلا مطر), or a candle that burns and illuminates its surroundings but destroys itself, or a beast tied to a "mill" (طاحونه) that constantly toils and travels, but because it moves in circles, it never covers any distance or reaches anywhere. These and similar analogies each illustrate a corner of the grim fate of the scholar without action.

In Islamic narrations, shocking expressions have also been used to condemn such scholars:

Among them: It is narrated from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) that he said: "Whoever increases in knowledge but does not increase in guidance, this knowledge yields nothing for him except distance from Allah" (من ازداد علما و لم يزدد هدى لم يزدد من اللَّه الا بعدا) [1]. Elsewhere, we read from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him): "Knowledge is coupled with action; whoever knows something must act upon it. Knowledge calls out to action; if it receives a positive response, it stays, otherwise, it departs!" (العلم مقرون بالعمل، فمن علم عمل، و العلم يهتف بالعمل فان اجابه و الا ارتحل عنه‌) [2].

In principle, it is understood from some narrations that a scholar without action does not deserve the title of "scholar." The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) says: "A man is not a scholar until he is a practitioner of his knowledge" (لا يكون المرء عالما حتى يكون بعلمه عاملا) [3]. Even more so: he carries the full responsibility of a scholar without enjoying the advantages of knowledge. Thus, in a tradition from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), we read that during a sermon on the pulpit, he said:

"O people! When you know, act upon what you have learned so that you may be guided. Indeed, the scholar who acts contrary to his knowledge is like the bewildered ignorant who never wakes from his ignorance; rather, I see that the proof against such a scholar is greater, and the regret more lasting." [1]

Undoubtedly, the existence of such scholars and scientists is considered the greatest calamity for a society, and the fate of a people whose scholars are like this is a dangerous one, as the poet says: "The shepherd of the sheep protects them from the wolf, but what if the shepherds themselves are wolves to them!"

2- Why Should I Fear Death?

Usually, most people fear death; only a small group smiles in the face of death and embraces it tightly, giving up a colorful cloak to receive an eternal soul.

But let us see why death, its manifestations, and even its name are painful for a group? The main reason is that they do not believe in life after death, or if they do, this faith has not become a deep conviction and has not governed their thoughts and emotions.

Man's terror of annihilation and non-existence is natural. Man even fears the darkness of night because darkness is the non-existence of light, and sometimes fears a corpse because

it too is on the path of annihilation.

But if a person believes with their whole being that the world is a prison for the believer and a paradise for the disbeliever ("الدنيا سجن المؤمن و جنة الكافر") [1]; if they believe that this earthly body is a cage for the bird of their soul, which, when broken, allows the soul to be free and fly toward the Beloved's abode;

If they believe that "the dust of the body becomes a veil over the soul's face," they will surely long for the moment when the veil is cast off from this face.

If they believe they are a bird of the celestial garden and not of this earthly world, and that only "for a few days, a cage has been made of their body"?

Yes, if a person's perspective on death is such, they will never be terrified of death, even while desiring life to traverse the path of perfection.

Thus, we read in the account of Ashura: the more the enemy's siege tightened and the pressure on Hussain (peace be upon him) and his companions increased, the more their faces became radiant and blooming. Even the elderly among his companions were smiling on the morning of Ashura. When asked why, they said: "Because in a few hours, we shall drink the nectar of martyrdom and embrace the Houris!" [2]

Another reason for the fear of death is excessive attachment to the world, because death creates a separation between the person and their beloved, and letting go of all the facilities provided for a comfortable and luxurious life is unbearable for them.

The third factor is the emptiness of the column of good deeds and the fullness of the column of sins in the record of actions.

In a tradition, we read that someone came to the Prophet and said: "O Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family), why do I not love death?"

He asked: "Do you have wealth?" He replied: "Yes." He asked: "Have you sent any of it ahead of you?" He replied: "No." He said: "It is for this reason that you do not love death (because your record of deeds is empty of good works)." [1]

Another person came to Abu Dharr and asked the same question: "Why do we detest death?!" He replied: "Because you have built up the world and ruined the Hereafter; so you hate to move from a place of prosperity to a place of ruin!" [2]

[1] & [2]: Al-Mahajjat al-Bayda, Vol. 8, p. 258.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا نُودِىَ لِلصَّلَوٰةِ مِن يَوْمِ ٱلْجُمُعَةِ فَٱسْعَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلْبَيْعَ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٩)
O you who have faith! When the call is made for prayer on Friday, hurry toward the remembrance of Allah, and leave all business. That is better for you, should you know. (9)
فَإِذَا قُضِيَتِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةُ فَٱنتَشِرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٠)
And when the prayer is finished disperse through the land and seek Allah’s grace, and remember Allah much so that you may be felicitous. (10)
وَإِذَا رَأَوْا۟ تِجَـٰرَةً أَوْ لَهْوًا ٱنفَضُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَآئِمًا ۚ قُلْ مَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهْوِ وَمِنَ ٱلتِّجَـٰرَةِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (١١)
When they sight a deal or a diversion, they scatter off towards it and leave you standing! Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion and dealing, and Allah is the best of providers.’ (11)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for these verses, or specifically the verse: "وَ إِذا رَأَوْا تِجارَةً", various narrations have been cited, all of which convey that in one of the years when the people of Medina were suffering from drought, hunger, and a hike in the prices of goods, "Dihyah" arrived from Sham with a caravan bringing food supplies. It was a Friday, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was busy delivering the Friday prayer sermon. As was customary, they beat drums and even played other musical instruments to announce the arrival of the caravan. The people rushed toward the market. At this moment, the Muslims who had gathered in the mosque for prayer abandoned the sermon and hurried toward the market to secure their needs. Only twelve men and one woman remained in the mosque (the verse was revealed and severely rebuked them). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "If this small group had also left, stones would have rained upon them from the sky." [^1]

Commentary

The Greatest Weekly Religious-Political Gathering

In the preceding verses, there were concise discussions regarding Monotheism, Prophethood, and the Hereafter, as well as a rebuke of the world-loving Jews. The verses under discussion address one of the most important Islamic duties which has an extraordinary effect on strengthening the foundations of faith and, in one sense, constitutes the primary objective of the Surah: the Friday Prayer (Salat al-Jumu'ah) and some of its rulings.

First, addressing all Muslims, He says: "O you who have believed! When the call is made for prayer on the day of Jumu'ah (Friday), hasten to the remembrance of Allah (the sermon and the prayer) and leave business. That is better for you, if you only knew" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا نُودِيَ لِلصَّلاةِ مِنْ يَوْمِ الْجُمُعَةِ فَاسْعَوْا إِلى‌ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ وَ ذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

"Nudiya" is derived from "Nida," meaning to raise a cry or call, and here it means the Adhan (call to prayer), for in Islam we have no call for prayer other than the Adhan, as we also read in verse 58 of Surah Al-Ma'idah: "And when you call to prayer (and recite the Adhan), they take it in ridicule and amusement. That is because they are a people who do not understand."

Thus, when the sound of the Friday prayer Adhan is raised, people are duty-bound to leave their business and trade and hasten toward the prayer, which is the most important remembrance of Allah.

The phrase "That is better for you..." indicates that establishing the Friday prayer and abandoning business at that time entails significant benefits for Muslims if they reflect upon it; otherwise, Allah is independent of all and is Compassionate toward all.

This phrase is a brief allusion to the philosophy and benefits of the Friday prayer, which, God willing, we will discuss in the "Key Points" section.

Of course, "leaving business and trade" has a broad meaning that includes any distracting activity.

As for why the day of "Jumu'ah" (Friday) was given this name, it is because of the gathering (ijtima') of people on this day for prayer, and this matter has a history that will be mentioned in the "Key Points."

It is noteworthy that in some Islamic narrations regarding the daily prayers, it is stated: "When the prayer (the daily prayers) is established, do not come to it running, but come to it walking, and maintain tranquility." [^1] However, regarding the Friday prayer, the above verse says: "Fas'aw" (hasten), and this is evidence of the extraordinary importance of the Friday prayer.

The meaning of "remembrance of Allah" (ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ‌) is primarily the "prayer," but we know that the Friday prayer sermons, which are infused with the remembrance of Allah, are in fact a part of the Friday prayer; therefore, one must also hasten to participate in them.

In the next verse, He adds: "Then when the prayer is concluded, you are free to disperse through the land and seek from the bounty of Allah, and remember Allah much that you may succeed" (فَإِذا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلاةُ فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ ابْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَ اذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ كَثِيراً لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ‌).

Although the phrase "seek from the bounty of Allah" or similar expressions in the Holy Quran are perhaps most often used to mean seeking sustenance, business, and trade, it is clear that the concept of this phrase is broad, and business is just one of its instances. Thus, some have interpreted it to mean visiting the sick, visiting a believer, or acquiring knowledge, though it is not limited to these either.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 287, and most other commentaries. [^1]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 28, p. 90.

It goes without saying that the command to "disperse through the land" and "seek sustenance" is not an obligatory command (wujub), but rather what is termed "a command after a prohibition" (amr ba'd al-hazr), which serves as evidence of permissibility (jawaz). However, some have inferred from this expression that seeking sustenance after the Friday prayer possesses a certain desirability and blessing. It is mentioned in a Hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would visit the market after the Friday prayer.

The phrase "and remember Allah much" refers to remembering Allah in gratitude for all the blessings He has bestowed upon man. Some have interpreted "remembrance" (dhikr) here to mean "reflection" (fikr), as it is stated in the Hadith: "One hour of reflection is better than a year of worship." [^1] Some have also interpreted it as the matter of being mindful of Allah in the markets and during transactions, and not deviating from the principles of truth and justice.

However, it is clear that the verse has a broad meaning that encompasses all of these. It is also certain that the soul of "remembrance" is "reflection," and remembrance without reflection is nothing more than a verbal habit. That which brings about success and salvation is remembrance coupled with reflection in all states.

Fundamentally, continuous "remembrance" causes the memory of Allah to penetrate the depths of the human soul and burns away the roots of negligence and heedlessness—which are the primary causes of every sin—placing man on the path of success and salvation, whereby the reality of "that you may succeed" is achieved.

In the final verse under discussion, those who abandoned the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله during the Friday prayer and rushed to the market to buy from the newly arrived caravan are severely rebuked: "And when they see a transaction or some amusement, they disperse to it and leave you standing (while you are delivering the Friday sermon)" (وَ إِذا رَأَوْا تِجارَةً أَوْ لَهْواً انْفَضُّوا إِلَيْها وَ تَرَكُوكَ قائِماً).

But "Say: What is with Allah is better than amusement and than transaction, and Allah is the best of providers" (قُلْ ما عِنْدَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللَّهْوِ وَ مِنَ التِّجارَةِ وَ اللَّهُ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ‌).

The divine reward and the blessings that come to you from attending the Friday prayer, hearing the exhortations and advice of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the spiritual and moral training are incomparable to anything else. And if you fear that your sustenance will be cut off, you are mistaken; Allah is the best of providers.

The term "amusement" (lahw) refers to the drums and other instruments of diversion that were beaten upon the arrival of a new caravan in Medina, which served both as a type of news and announcement and as a means of entertainment and advertising for the goods—just as examples of this are seen today in Western-style stores.

The expression "infaddu" means dispersing and turning away from the congregational prayer and turning toward the caravan. As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, when the caravan of "Dihyah" entered Medina (and he had not yet embraced Islam), he called the people to the market with the sound of drums and other instruments of amusement. The people of Medina, even the Muslims who were in the mosque listening to the Friday sermon of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, rushed toward him, and only thirteen people—or according to some narrations, fewer—remained in the mosque.

The pronoun in "ilayha" (to it) refers back to "transaction" (tijarah), meaning they rushed toward the merchandise. This is because amusement was not their primary goal, but rather a prelude to announcing the arrival of the caravan or entertainment and advertising alongside it.

The expression "qa'iman" (standing) shows that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله used to deliver the Friday sermon while standing, as narrated in a Hadith from "Jabir bin Samurah" who said: "I never saw the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله delivering a sermon while sitting, and whoever says he delivered the sermon sitting, then disbelieve him." [^1] It is also narrated that "Abdullah bin Mas'ud" was asked: "Did the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله deliver the sermon standing?" He said: "Have you not heard that Allah says: 'and they left you standing'?" [^2]

In the commentary "Durr al-Manthur," it is stated that the first person to deliver the Friday sermon while sitting was Mu'awiyah [^3]!

Key Points

1- The First Friday Prayer in Islam

In some Islamic narrations, it is stated that the Muslims of Medina, before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله emigrated, spoke with one another and said: "The Jews gather on one day of the week (Saturday), and the Christians also have a day for gathering (Sunday). It would be good if we also designated a day to gather and remember Allah and offer Him thanks." They chose the day before Saturday, which at that time was called "Yawm al-Arubah," for this purpose and went to "As'ad bin Zurarah" (one of the elders of Medina). He performed the prayer in congregation with them and advised them, and that day was named "Yawm al-Jumu'ah" (the Day of Gathering) because it was the day of the Muslims' gathering.

"As'ad" ordered a sheep to be slaughtered, and the lunch and dinner for everyone were from that single sheep, as the number of Muslims on that day was very small... And this was the first Friday prayer established in Islam.

However, the first Friday prayer that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله established with his companions was when he emigrated to Medina. He entered Medina on Monday, the twelfth of Rabi' al-Awwal at noon. The Prophet stayed in "Quba" for four days and founded the Quba Mosque. Then, on Friday, he moved toward Medina (the distance between Quba and Medina is very short, and today Quba is one of the neighborhoods within Medina). At the time of the Friday prayer, he reached the neighborhood of "Bani Salim" and established the Friday prayer ceremony there. This was the first Friday prayer the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله performed in Islam. He also delivered a sermon in this Friday prayer, which was his first sermon in Medina. [^1] One of the traditionists narrated from "Abdur-Rahman bin Ka'b" that: "Whenever my father heard the sound of the Friday Adhan, he would send mercy upon 'As'ad bin Zurarah.' When I asked for the reason, he said: 'Because he was the first person to perform the Friday prayer with us.' I asked: 'How many were you that day?' He said: 'Only forty people!'" [^2]

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 289. [^1] and [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 286. [^3]: "Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 222—this narration has been cited by other commentators such as "Alusi" in "Ruh al-Ma'ani" and "Qurtubi" in his Tafsir. [^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 286. [^2]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 28, p. 88.

2- The Importance of the Friday Prayer

The best evidence for the importance of this great Islamic obligation, before anything else, are the verses of this very Surah, which command all Muslims and people of faith to hasten toward it as soon as they hear the Friday Adhan, and to leave every business and distracting program—to the extent that even in a year when people are suffering from food shortages, if a caravan arrives carrying their needs, they should not go toward it but continue the Friday prayer program.

In Islamic Hadiths, numerous emphases have also been recorded in this regard, including: In a sermon narrated by both supporters and opponents from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "Indeed, Allah the Exalted has made the Friday prayer obligatory upon you. Whoever abandons it during my life or after my death, out of belittling it or denying it, may Allah not gather his affairs, and may He not bless him in his matter. Behold, his prayer is not accepted, behold, his Zakat is not accepted, behold, his Hajj is not accepted, behold, his fasting is not accepted, behold, his good deeds will not be accepted until he repents!" [^1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "The Friday prayer is an obligation, and gathering for it with the Imam is an obligation. If a man abandons three Friday prayers without an excuse, he has abandoned three obligations, and no one abandons three obligations without an excuse except a hypocrite." [^1] And in another Hadith from the Messenger of Allah, we read: "Whoever attends the Friday prayer out of faith and seeking reward, his deeds shall be started anew (his sins forgiven)." [^2] The narrations in this field are many, and mentioning all of them would be lengthy. We conclude this discussion by referring to one more Hadith.

A man came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Messenger of Allah! I have prepared for Hajj many times but have not been granted the success." He said: "Upon you is the Friday prayer, for it is the Hajj of the poor." (An allusion to the fact that many of the blessings of the great Islamic congress of Hajj exist in the gathering of the Friday prayer). [^3] Of course, it must be noted that the severe rebukes mentioned regarding the abandonment of the Friday prayer—where those who abandon it are counted among the hypocrites—apply when the Friday prayer is an individual obligation (Wajib 'Ayni), meaning during the time of the presence of the Infallible Imam who has political authority. However, during the time of Occultation, based on it being an optional obligation (Wajib Takhyiri—the choice between Friday prayer and Dhuhr prayer), and if it is not done out of belittling or denial, it will not be subject to these rebukes, although the greatness of the Friday prayer and its extraordinary importance are preserved even in this state (further explanation of this matter should be studied in books of jurisprudence).

3- The Philosophy of the Religious-Political Friday Prayer

The Friday prayer is, first and foremost, a great collective act of worship, and it carries the general effect of acts of worship, which is the refinement of the soul and spirit, the washing of the heart from the pollutions of sin, and the removal of the rust of disobedience from the heart—especially since it is preceded by two sermons containing various exhortations, advice, and commands to piety and God-wariness.

As for the social and political aspect, it is a great weekly congress which, after the annual Hajj congress, is the largest Islamic gathering. For this reason, in the narration previously cited from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it was stated that Friday is the Hajj of those who are unable to participate in the Hajj ceremonies.

In reality, Islam gives importance to three great gatherings: 1. Daily gatherings achieved in the congregational prayer. 2. The weekly gathering in the Friday prayer ceremony. 3. The Hajj gathering which takes place once a year at the House of Allah.

The role of the Friday prayer in this context is very important, especially since one of the programs of the speaker (Khatib) in the Friday prayer sermon is to mention important political, social, and economic issues. Thus, this magnificent and glorious gathering can be the source of the following blessings: A) Educating the people regarding Islamic knowledge and important social and political events. B) Creating greater solidarity and cohesion among the ranks of Muslims in a way that strikes fear into the enemies and makes them tremble. C) Renewing the religious spirit and spiritual vitality for the masses of Muslim people. D) Enlisting cooperation to solve public problems.

For this reason, the enemies of Islam have always feared a Friday prayer that meets all the criteria and in which Islamic instructions are precisely observed.

Also for this reason, the Friday prayer has always been a powerful political lever in the hands of governments; however, just governments like that of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله utilized it for the best interests of Islam, while oppressive governments like the Umayyads misused it to consolidate the foundations of their power.

Throughout history, we observe instances where anyone who wanted to rise against a government would first refrain from attending its Friday prayer. As we read in the story of Ashura, a group of Shiites gathered in the house of "Sulayman bin Surad al-Khuza'i" and sent a letter to Imam Hussain عليه السلام from Kufa, stating: "Nu'man bin Bashir, the Umayyad governor of Kufa, has been isolated and we do not participate in his Friday prayer, and if we know that you are moving toward us, we will expel him from Kufa." [^1] In the Sahifa Sajjadiya, we read from Imam Sajjad Ali bin al-Hussain عليه السلام: "O Allah! This station (referring to the Friday and Eid prayers) belongs to Your caliphs and Your chosen ones, and it is the place of Your high-ranking trustees whom You have distinguished with it, but they (the oppressive Umayyad caliphs) have seized it by force." [^2] Sometimes it happens that the enemies of Islam engage in poisonous propaganda for an entire week, day and night, but it is all neutralized by a single Friday prayer sermon and its glorious, life-giving ceremonies; a new spirit is breathed into the bodies, and fresh blood begins to flow in the veins.

Considering the point that according to Shiite jurisprudence, more than one Friday prayer is not permissible within a radius of one Farsakh by one Farsakh (approx. 5.5 km), and even those located two Farsakhs (approx. 11 km) away from the place of the Friday prayer participate in it, it becomes clear that practically in every small or large city and its suburbs, no more than one Friday prayer will be held. Therefore, such a gathering constitutes the largest assembly of that region.

But with great regret, this religious-political ceremony, which could be the starting point of a great movement in Islamic societies, has become so soulless and listless in some Islamic countries due to the influence of corrupt governments that practically no positive effect is derived from it, and it has taken the form of a ceremonial program. Truly, this is one of the great assets over whose loss one must weep.

The most important Friday prayer of the year is the one performed in Mecca before going to Arafat, in which all the pilgrims of the House of Allah from all over the world participate—who are the true representatives of all strata of Muslims on Earth. It is fitting that for the preparation of the sermon of such a sensitive prayer, a large number of scholars should study for weeks and months and present the product of that on that sensitive day and historic sermon to the Muslims. Certainly, by its blessing, they could provide much awareness to the Islamic society and solve major problems.

But with great regret, in these times, it is seen that very trivial issues and matters that almost everyone is aware of are raised, and there is absolutely no news of fundamental issues. Should one not weep for the loss of such great opportunities and immense assets?! And should one not rise to transform it?!

[^1]: "Wasa'il al-Shi'ah," Vol. 5, p. 7, Chapter on the Obligation of Friday Prayer, Hadith 28. [^1]: "Wasa'il al-Shi'ah," Vol. 5, p. 4, Hadith 8. [^2]: Ibid, Hadith 10. [^3]: Ibid, p. 5, Hadith 17. [^1]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 44, p. 333. [^2]: "Sahifa Sajjadiya," Supplication 48.

4- Etiquettes of Friday Prayer and the Content of the Sermons

The Friday prayer (given the necessary conditions) is obligatory upon adult, healthy men who have the ability to participate, and it is not obligatory upon travelers and the elderly, although participation in the Friday prayer is permissible for the traveler. Likewise, women can participate in the Friday prayer, although it is not obligatory upon them.

The minimum number with which the Friday prayer is established is five men.

The Friday prayer consists of two units (Rak'ahs) and takes the place of the Dhuhr prayer, and the two sermons delivered before the Friday prayer are in fact considered in place of the other two units.

The Friday prayer is like the Fajr prayer, and it is recommended (Mustahabb) to recite the Hamd and the Surah aloud. It is also recommended to recite Surah "Al-Jumu'ah" in the first unit and Surah "Al-Munafiqun" in the second unit.

Two Qunuts are recommended in the Friday prayer: one before the Ruku' of the first unit and the other after the Ruku' of the second unit.

Delivering two sermons before the Friday prayer is obligatory, just as it is obligatory for the speaker (Khatib) to stand while delivering the sermon. The person who delivers the sermon must be the same Imam of the Friday prayer.

The Khatib must raise his voice so that the people hear him, so that the content of the sermon reaches everyone's ears.

During the delivery of the sermon, one must remain silent, listen to the words of the Khatib, and sit facing the Khatib.

It is fitting for the Khatib to be an eloquent and articulate man, aware of the circumstances and conditions of the Muslims, informed about the interests of the Islamic society, brave, plain-spoken, and decisive in expressing the truth. His actions and behavior should cause his words to be influential, and his life should remind people of Allah.

It is fitting for him to wear the cleanest clothes, perfume himself, and walk with dignity and tranquility. When he takes his place upon the pulpit (Minbar), he should greet the people, stand facing them, and lean on a sword, a bow, or a staff. He should first sit on the pulpit until the Adhan is finished, and after the completion of the Adhan, begin the sermon.

The content of the sermon first includes the praise of Allah and blessings upon the Prophet (precaution dictates that this part be in Arabic, but the rest may be recited in the language of the listeners). Then he should advise the people to have Taqwa (God-wariness) and recite one of the short Surahs of the Quran, observing this in both sermons. In the second sermon, after blessings upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, he should pray for the Imams of the Muslims and seek forgiveness for the believing men and women.

It is fitting that within the sermon he raises important issues related to the religion and worldly affairs of the Muslims, and discusses what is needed by the Muslims inside and outside Islamic countries and within and outside that region. He should raise political, social, economic, and religious issues considering priorities, provide awareness to the people, inform them of the conspiracies of the enemies, and remind them of short-term and long-term programs for the preservation of the Islamic society and the neutralization of the plans of opponents.

In short, the Khatib must be very alert, awake, and a person of thought and study in Islamic matters, making maximum use of the opportunity of this great ceremony to advance the goals of Islam and the Muslims. [^1]

In a Hadith from Imam Ali bin Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read: "The sermon was legislated on the day of Friday because Friday is a general gathering. Thus, He wanted the leader of the Muslims to have a means to exhort them, encourage them toward obedience, frighten them from disobedience, and inform them of what He intends for the benefit of their religion and their world, and inform them of the news and alarming events reaching him from the horizons which contain harm or benefit for them... And two sermons were established so that one is for the praise of Allah, glorification, and sanctification of Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, and the other is for needs, warnings, supplications, and what he wishes to teach them of His commands and prohibitions that contain benefit or corruption." [^1]

5- Conditions for the Obligation of Friday Prayer

There is no doubt that the Friday Imam, like any other congregational Imam, must be just ('Adil), but the question is whether he has conditions beyond this or not?

A group believes that this prayer is among the duties of the Infallible Imam or his specific representative; in other words, it pertains to the time of the presence of the Infallible Imam عليه السلام.

While many researchers believe that this is a condition for the individual obligation (Wajib 'Ayni) of the Friday prayer, but for the optional obligation (Wajib Takhyiri), this condition is not necessary, and the Friday prayer can be established during the time of Occultation as well, taking the place of the Dhuhr prayer. This is the correct view; rather, whenever an Islamic government with its conditions is formed by the general deputy of the Imam عليه السلام, precaution dictates that the Friday Imam be appointed by him, and Muslims participate in the Friday prayer.

In this regard and in other matters related to the Friday prayer, there is much to say, the mention of all of which is beyond the scope of a commentary discussion and must be sought in books of jurisprudence and Hadith. [^1]

O Allah! Grant us the success to make maximum use of these great Islamic symbols for the training of souls and the salvation of Muslims from the clutches of enemies. O Lord! Make us among those who long for Your meeting and do not fear death. O God! Never strip from us the blessing of faith and the teaching and training of the Prophets.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Jumu'ah 14 Ramadan 1406

[^1]: Of course, regarding the specifics and details of the rulings of Friday prayer and the sermons, there are minor differences in the fatwas of the jurists, and what was mentioned above is a summary of various fatwas. [^1]: "Wasa'il al-Shi'ah," Vol. 5, p. 39. [^1]: The late Allamah Majlisi in "Bihar al-Anwar," Volumes 89 and 90, has addressed this important issue and other characteristics of Friday.
